name: grace_full_lifecycle_sensor_still_open
description: Warm day. Sensor opens at 13:00 (pause). Sensor closes at 13:10 (grace starts, 5-min grace). At 13:12 sensor opens again while grace is active — grace blocks re-pause. Grace expires at 13:15 with sensor still open → re-pause fires.
notes: Tests the grace period full lifecycle: pause → grace → sensor opens during grace (blocked) → re-pause on grace expiry.
Issue #476: migrated to use_coordinator=true, same pattern as grace_full_lifecycle_clean_expiry.json.
sensor_open/sensor_close dispatch via fake_hass.states.async_set() through the real
_async_door_window_changed listener; synthetic grace_start marker removed (real grace flows from
the sensor_close dispatch's own async_call_later timer). grace_end is KEPT as a no-op dispatch —
its only remaining role is forcing scheduler.advance_to(13:15:00) so the real grace timer gets a
chance to fire; without an event at that time the scheduler never advances there.
The 13:12 assertion was 'no_action' in the legacy-simulator version. outcomes.py's
production_outcome_at() has no 'no_action' sentinel — it always carries forward the most recent
REAL decision at or before the timestamp (returns 'no_decision' only if there is none at all), so
'no_action' as a literal expected string could never match. Corrected to 'resumed' — the carried-
forward outcome from 13:10 — which is the correct way to assert 'no NEW decision fired here' against
this harness: if grace incorrectly failed to block the reopen and a new pause fired, the outcome at
13:12 would become 'paused', not 'resumed', and this assertion would correctly fail.
skip_startup_coalesce=true required (see away_setpoint_change_not_override.json).
Migrating this scenario found a real bug (fixed in run_production.py, not production code):
run_production_scenario() unconditionally overwrote engine._sensor_check_callback with a
_SensorTracker stub that coordinator-mode sensor dispatch never updates, clobbering the real
coordinator._any_sensor_open wiring set during __init__. This silently broke ANY check depending
on 'is a sensor still open' via the callback — specifically _on_grace_expired's re-pause decision
always saw 'nothing open' regardless of real sensor state. Fixed: only wire the tracker in
engine-only mode; coordinator-mode scenarios keep the real callback untouched.
Revert test: temporarily removed the _grace_active guard in handle_door_window_open
(automation.py) — the 13:12 assertion correctly failed (sensor_opened fired immediately,
outcome became 'paused' instead of the expected carried-forward 'resumed'). Restored; git diff
confirmed clean. Confirms this scenario genuinely exercises the grace-blocks-reopen guard.

events.note: Hot afternoon. Indoor above comfort_cool=74F. Outdoor too warm for nat vent.
Back door opens. Outdoor 88F > indoor 76F — nat vent not eligible. Automation pauses.
Door closes. All sensors closed — automation resumes and grace period begins (5 min = 300s).
Door opens again at 13:12, while grace is still active (expires at 13:15). Grace should block re-pause.

No-op dispatch (grace_end is not a real production event type) — kept purely to force scheduler.advance_to(13:15:00), the moment the REAL grace timer (started by the 13:10:00 sensor_close, manual_grace_seconds=300) is due. Without an event here the scheduler would never advance far enough for the real async_call_later callback to fire.

assertions.reason: Sensor opens with outdoor (88F) > indoor (76F) — nat vent ineligible. Automation pauses.
All sensors closed — automation resumes. Grace period begins.

Sensor opens during active grace period — grace blocks re-pause, so the outcome at 13:12 is still the carried-forward 'resumed' from 13:10 (no new decision fired). Corrected from legacy 'no_action' — see notes.

Grace expires with sensor still open → real _on_grace_expired() re-pause fires (grace_expired event with re_paused=True, mapped to outcome 'paused'), which ALSO schedules _apply_current_scheduled_state() as a follow-up task (automation.py) — since the sensor is still open, this correctly suppresses re-applying classification and emits classification_suppressed_paused at the SAME timestamp, which is the last (and thus reported) decision. This is a stronger confirmation than the legacy simulator could produce: not just 'grace re-paused' but 'the re-pause genuinely blocks classification, verified via the real code path that runs after grace resolves.' Corrected from 'paused' during #476 migration once the actual last-decision tie was traced.

verdict.summary: Grace period blocks re-pause during active window; grace expiry with open sensor triggers re-pause
verdict.observed_behavior: paused (13:00) → resumed (13:10) → grace blocks re-pause (13:12) → re-paused on grace expiry (13:15)
verdict.expected_behavior: Grace period prevents rapid pause/resume oscillation; re-pause fires if sensor is still open when grace expires
